title: TBM 409: Minimally Viable Consistency (Part 2)
questions: If we removed this consistency requirement, what is the worst thing that could happen?
What specific risk are we trying to reduce with consistency (confusion, coordination failures, reporting gaps, regulatory risk, etc.)?
Does this consistency make people’s lives easier or harder, and who benefits versus who pays the cost?
Is the consistency a temporary scaffold with an expiration date, and what would it take to remove it later?
summary: This post explores how to design a company “operating system” with minimally viable consistency—enough shared language and interfaces to coordinate, but not so much that it creates cognitive load or premature convergence. It’s aimed at product/engineering/design/data leaders and managers involved in enablement, product operations, or strategic finance during operating model transformations, especially when strategy and structure are temporarily out of sync. It also provides decision questions for judging whether consistency is truly viable, gracefully adaptable, and worth keeping as a temporary scaffold.

title: TBM 395: Words! Damned Words!
questions: When do different definitions of the same work noun (initiative, bet, program) become an actual organizational problem?
How can we reduce semantic drift and ontological conflict without forcing everyone into one shared vocabulary?
What happens when governance and accounting models mistake abstractions for reality in product development?
How should we decide what to coordinate and fund across the org when boundaries between concepts are fuzzy?
search_terms: initiative vs bet vs project definitions
summary: This post explores how organizational work nouns (like initiatives, bets, and capabilities) can either help coordination or cause failure when meanings collide across strategy, finance, and delivery. It’s for product and organizational leaders who care about language, governance, and the practical mechanics of prioritization—especially when semantic ambiguity affects incentives, funding, and decision-making. The author argues for grounding alignment in events and designing systems that tolerate multiple frames rather than enforcing one “correct” definition.

title: TBM 393: Why Labeling Relationships Is So Important
questions: How should goals and initiatives relate in an operating model (anchor, derived, constrained, or aggregated)?
What worldview is encoded by different strategy-to-execution models (linear decomposition vs learning-loop bets)?
Why do hierarchical scaling approaches like SAFe-style rollups or cascading OKRs fail at enterprise scale?
How can mapping an organization as a semantic network or lightweight ontology improve understanding of work?
search_terms: strategy to execution bets vs commitments
summary: The post argues that organizations should be modeled by explicitly labeling the relationships between work concepts (edges carry meaning), not by relying on tidy hierarchies. It helps leaders and strategy practitioners compare competing mental models for how goals and initiatives connect, and it explains why scaling agile and goal cascades often break due to changing dynamics at larger distances. The intended audience is people designing or diagnosing operating models who want better conceptual tools for mapping how work actually flows.

title: TBM 375: Minimally Viable Consistency
questions: Where do we critically need shared language, norms, and expectations to coordinate effectively?
Where is common language actually holding us back by being too vague, overloaded, or mismatched to reality?
What is the least risky, highest-leverage way to introduce consistency in our context (words, norms, habits, or learning methods)?
How do we decide when to loosen consistency to support a product-oriented operating model?
search_terms: side systems caused by misfit process
summary: The post introduces “minimally viable consistency” as a principle for designing a company operating system with the smallest set of shared concepts and language that still works in practice. It’s aimed at leaders and team designers who struggle with alignment, overloaded terminology, or coordination breakdowns, and need a context-sensitive way to decide when to standardize and when to loosen. The examples (OKRs/pillars, launch-release calendars, and overloaded terms like “initiatives”) illustrate how consistency can either reduce friction or force teams into unhelpful workarounds.

title: TBM 365: The Problem With Value Hierarchies (Video)
questions: Why do value hierarchies (pillars to tasks) fail to represent real strategy and learning?
How can teams model both outcomes and execution across different time horizons?
What artifact types and cadences best support a networked operating model?
Which rituals help resolve contradictions across strategy, finance, structure, and work lenses?
summary: This post critiques the common “value hierarchy” pyramid used for strategy-to-task planning, arguing it misrepresents how information, outcomes, and time-to-impact actually flow in organizations. It’s aimed at leaders and product/strategy teams designing operating models, proposing a multi-lens, networked approach using artifact types (anchor/intent/context) and rituals to keep the system coherent over time.

title: TBM 361: Context, Collaboration, Intent, and Investment
questions: Where should we invest more time in establishing foundational models that stay useful over time?
Does our company have a shared language and approach for funding and investment?
Do we feel like we’re chasing our tails each quarter, and what remains stable across cycles?
How well does strategic intent “stick” in our organization, and is it the right level of specificity?
search_terms: capability trees customer journey KPI trees
summary: The post proposes a practical operating-system framework—context, intent, collaboration, and investment—to help teams distinguish what they know, what they aim for, how people coordinate, and how resources are allocated over time. It’s aimed at leaders and product/strategy practitioners who struggle with oversimplified distinctions like strategy vs execution and want to reduce “hamster wheel” quarterly churn by building durable, shared models.

title: TBM 353: Conversation Quality and Scale
questions: How mature are our conversations and interactions, and what level of reflexive integration are we actually operating at?
Where should we focus to improve conversation quality, and is that focus strategic rather than just aspirational?
Are we confusing autonomy with isolation, leading to coordination failures between teams?
Are our conversation problems primarily skill gaps, or are they driven by incentives, trust, feedback, fear, or missing environmental support?
summary: This post proposes a way to assess the “maturity” of organizational conversations and interactions, treating them as signals of an organization’s operating system. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners who want to diagnose conversation quality, decide where to focus, and understand how scale, integration, and scaffolding affect whether good local interactions become reliable org-wide coordination. It also argues that conversation gaps are often systemic (feedback, goals, trust, incentives, fear, environment) rather than purely training problems.

title: TBM 348: Shared Understanding At Scale
questions: How can we make high-volume organizational information legible and actionable for leaders?
What views or dashboards should we build if there is no single universal big picture?
Where are our goal trees, roadmaps, or loops over-nested and producing “worthless” middle layers?
Which models are shaping what gets valued and resourced in our organization—and what do they leave out?
summary: This post explores how organizations can create shared understanding at scale by improving the way information is structured, filtered, and presented to decision-makers. It argues against universal dashboards and over-nested goal/loop structures, emphasizing task-coupled, role-specific views and ritualized shared interfaces. It also warns that common frameworks and models compress reality in ways that can distort power, attention, and resourcing—useful for leaders, product/strategy teams, and org designers.

title: TBM 347: "Why Don't Our Leaders Care About How We Work?"
questions: Why do leaders resist caring about how people work?
Is operating system design only possible in rare, founder-led cases?
How can I get leadership to take a more intentional approach to ways of working?
What should I do when chiefs of staff or program managers try to redesign the company too quickly?
search_terms: why leaders don't care about how we work
middle-out push fails global vs local
chief of staff redesigns operating model
summary: This post addresses why leadership teams often resist intentional design of a company’s ways of working, even when they value accountability and agency. It’s aimed at practitioners trying to influence operating system design inside organizations—especially when leaders treat “process” as beneath them, lack the relevant skill set, or delegate OS work to chiefs of staff too quickly. The guidance focuses on behavior-based framing, storytelling, partnership, and accounting for inertia and local autonomy.

title: TBM 344: Context, Possibility, Intent, and Action
questions: What rituals, artifacts, and cadences should we use to align possibility, intent, action, and context?
Where is our company getting the intent mix wrong across time horizon, procedural vs outcome orientation, concreteness, and leverage?
How should we adjust control and integration mechanisms as we move from startup scale to larger-company scale?
What should we do when context fragmentation leads to incoherent decisions and “not strategic enough” blame?
search_terms: team operating system possibility intent action context
intent types short-term long-term procedural outcome concrete abstract high leverage
control and integration cadence for organizations
portfolio of bets for future growth
summary: The post proposes a practical “operating system” lens for teams and organizations, built from five elements: possibility, intent, action, context, and control/integration. It argues that strategy and execution fail when intent signifiers are mixed incorrectly, and it offers a spectrum of intent types to add nuance beyond generic planning cascades. It’s aimed at leaders and operators designing rituals, artifacts, and cadences—especially when scaling from startup simplicity to larger-company complexity.

title: TBM 309: Zombie Practices and Processes
questions: Why do OKRs fail in large companies even when they work in small teams?
How does a practice drift from its original purpose into a zombie process?
What incentives and organizational dynamics cause OKRs to become performance-management paperwork?
What should leaders do to fight mission drift and restore usefulness to teams?
summary: This post explains why OKRs (and similar management practices) often degrade into “zombie processes” in large organizations, despite being straightforward in theory. It is aimed at product, engineering, and leadership teams trying to restore strategic focus and alignment by addressing mission drift, incentive misalignment, and the behavioral conditions that make practices stick or fail.

title: TBM 300: How to Think About Operations and Enablement
questions: What is the practical difference between operations and enablement in product and design contexts?
How do you decide what to standardize versus what to keep local and experimental?
How do you design enablement so it supports without over-enabling?
How can operations and enablement be managed as a unified capability system with maturation over time?
search_terms: service design thinking for internal teams
summary: This post helps operations and enablement leaders distinguish and integrate operations (operationalization) and enablement (equipping and support) in product, design, and broader organizational contexts. It argues for a holistic, platform-like view of these functions across functional, cross-functional, and general domains, unified through a capability-based framework. It is aimed at practitioners who want practical principles, experimentation guidance, and a strategy-to-tactics approach for building resilient organizational capabilities.

title: TBM 251: Scale vs. Efficiency
questions: When is strategic inefficiency worth the gamble?
What are good examples of companies that won by being slower or less efficient than competitors?
How can you distinguish normal growing pains from core efficiency failures in your organization?
What clues indicate that a team’s strategic inefficiency is actually going the wrong direction?
search_terms: economies of scale and operational excellence
summary: This post explores how scale and operational efficiency interact, arguing that efficiency declines with growth are not inevitable and that investor outcomes can be driven by factors beyond day-to-day efficiency. It introduces a distinction between normal growing pains and “core efficiency,” then explains how teams may intentionally tolerate certain inefficiencies to pursue higher-priority strategic bets. The piece is aimed at leaders and operators navigating growth, prioritization, and execution tradeoffs in scaling organizations.

title: TBM 205: "Process" vs. Systems & Habits
questions: How do we distinguish valuable ways of working from performative, non-value-add process?
What incentives cause teams to fall into reactive planning and learned helplessness?
How can leaders build habits and systems that enable autonomy while staying aligned to company strategy?
Why do big-bang offsites fail to sustain improvements after returning to business as usual?
search_terms: how to break a wicked loop in organizations
summary: The post contrasts two organizational approaches to planning: a compliance-driven, annual “process” that produces performative rushing versus a habit-based “system” that continuously shapes ideas and keeps roadmaps aligned. It’s aimed at leaders and teams trying to improve strategy execution, morale, and adaptability by replacing reactive governance with human-centric operating habits and incremental change.

title: TBM 41/51: Why Goal Cascades are Harmful (and What to Do Instead)
questions: Why do goal cascades lead to misalignment and reduced congruence across teams?
How can we set goals without tying them to the org chart hierarchy?
What should goals be based on: hierarchical priorities or driver models and leverage points?
How do we distinguish inputs, outputs, and measurement when designing a goal system?
search_terms: network of goals instead of hierarchy
summary: The post argues that hierarchical goal cascades (few top-level goals replicated fractally) often create misalignment by reinforcing org boundaries, oversimplifying variable relationships, and conflating inputs/outputs with structure. It recommends an alternative for strategy and planning: build an org-agnostic driver model with measurement, identify leverage points, and then set goals based on the model (potentially with multiple layers and time horizons). The guidance is aimed at leaders and teams designing goal-setting and operating mechanisms for organizational congruence.

title: TBM 15/52: Stop Hiring Things to Do Too Many Jobs
questions: Are we overburdening this framework, tool, or label with too many purposes?
Do new team members understand the nuance and intent behind our operating artifacts?
How can we tell whether our OKRs or segmentation shorthand is creating alignment or confusion?
What test can we run to measure whether our internal messaging is convergent?
search_terms: stop hiring things to do too many jobs
anti-pattern frameworks doing too many jobs
summary: This post argues that adopting a framework, tool, or label to serve too many different purposes creates confusion, semantic drift, and weak alignment—especially for new joiners. It uses OKRs and a customer-segment label as examples and proposes a simple diagnostic: ask multiple people why you use the artifact and check how convergent their answers are.
